title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies

input_row.title: Ensure that the CNI in use supports Network Policies
input_row.description: There are a variety of CNI plugins available for Kubernetes. If the CNI in use does not support Network Policies it may not be possible to effectively restrict traffic in the cluster.
input_row.rationale: Kubernetes network policies are enforced by the CNI plugin in use. As such it is important to ensure that the CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress network policies. Impact: None
input_row.audit: Review the documentation of CNI plugin in use by the cluster, and confirm that it supports Ingress and Egress network policies.
input_row.remediation: If the CNI plugin in use does not support network policies, consideration should be given to making use of a different plugin, or finding an alternate mechanism for restricting traffic in the Kubernetes cluster. Default Value: This will depend on the CNI plugin in use. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.
input_row.references: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage- net/network-plugins/ Additional Information: One example here is Flannel (https://github.com/coreos/flannel) which does not support Network policy unless Calico is also in use.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires determining whether the installed CNI plugin supports both Ingress and Egress Network Policies. Kubernetes itself does not expose the CNI plugin type or its capabilities via the Kubernetes API or kubectl. There is no standard Kubernetes resource or field that definitively lists the CNI plugin in use or its feature set. While you can sometimes infer the CNI from pod labels or DaemonSets, confirming support for network policies (especially both Ingress and Egress) requires consulting the CNI plugin’s documentation or deployment manifests, which is not always possible via the Kubernetes API alone. Therefore, this check requires manual review.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. Identify the CNI plugin in use:
   - Review the manifests in the kube-system namespace for DaemonSets or Pods named after common CNI plugins (e.g., calico, weave, flannel, cilium, etc.).
   - Alternatively, check the cluster provisioning documentation or cloud provider configuration for the specified CNI.
2. Consult the official documentation for the identified CNI plugin.
   - Verify that the plugin explicitly states support for both Ingress and Egress Network Policies.
   - Example: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/extend-kubernetes/compute-storage-net/network-plugins/
3. Confirm that the CNI plugin is deployed and running in the cluster.
   - Use kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system to ensure the CNI DaemonSet is present and all pods are running.
4. Document your findings:
   - Record the CNI plugin name, version, and documentation reference confirming support for Network Policies.
   - If the plugin does not support both Ingress and Egress Network Policies, mark this as non-compliant.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.9.0 PDF
